content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Steve Piercy 
Website Builder 
3003 Fairway Drive 
Soquel, CA 95073 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-16-174 
Dear Mr. Piercy: 
October 5, 20 I 6 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the campaign provisions of the 
Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 This letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political 
Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders 
assistance. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Please note that our advice is based solely on 
the provisions of the Act, and nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct that 
may have already taken place. 
QUESTION 
May a campaign committee that uses payment card processors for contributions made via 
the Internet maintain a record of only the last four digits of a credit card number in lieu of the entire 
number? 
CONCLUSION 
Yes, because industry standards and laws that protect against credit card fraud restrict the 
disclosure, use, and storage of full credit card numbers. 
FACTS 
You are the website builder for a ballot measure committee that would like to use payment 
card processors, (such as PayPal), to receive contributions made via the Internet. You propose 
retaining in your records only the last four digits of a credit card number in lieu of the entire 
number. 
In order to use payment card processors, "merchants" (such as the committee) must agree to 
comply with the Payment Card Industry Data Security Standards (PCI DSS). Also, banks that issue 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-16-174 
Page No. 2 
credit cards are subject to laws limiting their use, storage, and disclosure of cardholder infonnation. 
Industry standards and security laws require most merchants receive only the last four digits of a 
credit card number, a security measure called truncation, unless they can ensure the security of this 
infonnation consistent with the PCI DSS and various other banking and financial laws governing 
the use and storage of credit card information. 
Compliance with these standards and laws is extremely rigorous, expensive, and must be 
done under an entity's strict control. For example, if a merchant retains credit card information, the 
numbers must be encrypted, securely stored to prevent data breaches, and stored in unreadable 
fonn. The use of strong one-way hash, truncation, index tokens and pads with secure storage of 
pads are pennitted. 
In addition, there are best practices in the industry to protect consumers from credit card 
fraud. For example, the Open Web Application Security Project regularly identifies the most critical 
web application security risks and provides a description, example vulnerabilities, example attacks 
and guidance on how to avoid opportunities for fraud and theft. Also, banks and other financial 
institutions must comply with various federal laws governing the use and storage of credit card 
infonnation and which ultimately restrict the use of this infonnation by credit card processors and 
merchants. 
ANALYSIS 
Candidates, treasurers, principal officers and elected officers are required to maintain 
detailed accounts, records, bills and receipts necessary to prepare campaign statements, to establish 
that campaign statements were properly filed, and to otherwise comply with the provisions of the 
Act. (Section 84104 ). This includes maintenance of original source documentation. (Regulation 
1840 I (a).) For contributions that are made through electronic transactions via the Internet, the 
original source documentation must include a record of the transaction created and transmitted by 
the cardholder, including the name of the cardholder, the cardholder's address and the card number, 
(Regulation 18401 (a)(2)(B).) 
At the time that the recordkeeping regulations applicable to electronic contributions were 
adopted in 2004, the use of payment card processors for transferring money was not contemplated. 
Since that time, as the online payment systems industry grew, campaign committees have 
increasingly used this payment method for online contributions. Also, widespread credit card fraud 
and identity theft have caused governments, banks, and other businesses to take measures to 
minimize opportunities for such crimes. We must therefore determine whether our recordkeeping 
regulations can accommodate the industry standards and laws restricting the use and storage of 
cardholder information. 
You state that, except for the full credit card numbers, all other infonnation and records that 
must be retained under Regulation 18401(a) are available to and can be stored by the committee. 
You also state that industry standards and laws permit merchants to receive only the last four digits 
of a credit card number, a security measure called truncation. It is apparent that a committee cannot 
retain information it does not have and cannot obtain. Therefore, if we find that retention of the 
truncated card number does not comply with the recordkeeping requirements, we effectively 
prohibit committees from using this payment method. We believe that applying today's industry 


File No. A-16-174 
Page No. 3 
standards and laws regarding cardholder information to campaign contributions is consistent with 
our recordkeeping requirements which are derived from the Act's purpose of disclosure so that 
"voters may be fully informed and improper practices may be inhibited." (Section 81002(a).) 
Therefore, we find that retention of only the last four digits of a credit card number satisfies 
the requirement in Regulation 18401 (a) that credit card numbers be retained. Of course, if during an 
audit or investigation additional information may be needed, we expect that the committee will 
assist us in obtaining that information. 
VJ:jgl 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
By: 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
General Counsel 
UCWJnQ.LI I  
Valentina Joyce 
Counsel, Legal Division

sections.question:
May a campaign committee that uses payment card processors for contributions made via 
the Internet maintain a record of only the last four digits of a credit card number in lieu of the entire 
number?

Yes, because industry standards and laws that protect against credit card fraud restrict the 
disclosure, use, and storage of full credit card numbers.

QUESTION: May a campaign committee that uses payment card processors for contributions made via 
the Internet maintain a record of only the last four digits of a credit card number in lieu of the entire 
number?

CONCLUSION: Yes, because industry standards and laws that protect against credit card fraud restrict the 
disclosure, use, and storage of full credit card numbers.
